query: can profound hypotension lead to myocardial ischemia in patients with coronary artery disease?

retrieved_docs.title: Coronary Perfusion Pressure -- Clinical Significance -- Coronary Perfusion Pressure and Type 2 Myocardial Infarction
retrieved_docs.content: Type 1 myocardial infarction involves the rupture of a coronary atherosclerotic plaque, leading to thrombus formation and stenosis of the arterial lumen. In contrast, type 2 myocardial infarction does not result from plaque rupture but from an imbalance between myocardial oxygen supply and demand. Decreased myocardial oxygen delivery can occur due to hypotension and reduced CPP, systemic hypoxia, or anemia. Increased myocardial oxygen demand may be caused by elevated afterload or tachyarrhythmias.

retrieved_docs.title: Coronary Perfusion Pressure -- Clinical Significance -- Coronary Perfusion Pressure and Type 2 Myocardial Infarction
retrieved_docs.content: Type 2 myocardial infarction can be multifactorial. For instance, tachyarrhythmias can raise oxygen demand and reduce stroke volume, leading to hypotension and CPP reduction. Patients with preexisting cardiac conditions, such as coronary artery disease or myocardial hypertrophy, rely on CPP and are less tolerant of reductions in CPP and myocardial oxygen delivery. [20] Types 1 and 2 myocardial infarctions have similar mortality rates. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Anesthetic Considerations in the Geriatric Population -- Issues of Concern -- Cardiovascular
retrieved_docs.content: Coronary atherosclerosis is not a component of normal aging and is rarely present in a healthy geriatric patient. However, elderly patients display a higher prevalence of coronary artery disease and are at higher risk for perioperative coronary ischemia. Left ventricular hypertrophy, widened pulse pressure with decreased diastolic blood pressure, and chronic hyperglycemia synergistically increases myocardial oxygen demand and decrease coronary perfusion. From the age of 65 to 86, the annual incidence of myocardial infarction in patients with no history of a cardiac event increases from 7.8 per 1000 person-years to 25.6, with higher risk associated with male sex, uncontrolled glucose levels, and poorly-treated hypertension. [6] [7] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Coronary Perfusion Pressure -- Clinical Significance -- Coronary Perfusion Pressure and Myocardial Infarction
retrieved_docs.content: Various factors influence CPP in myocardial infarction. Hypotension due to shock, arrhythmias, or excessive vasodilation can lower ADP and reduce CPP. Acute left ventricular dysfunction resulting from ischemia or infarction can increase LVEDP, further decreasing CPP. Additionally, tachycardia arising from myocardial infarction shortens diastole, reducing CPP.

retrieved_docs.content: Myocardial infarction or ischemia Anomalous coronary artery origin Coronary spasm Vasculitides Myocardial bridging

retrieved_docs.title: Coronary Perfusion Pressure -- Clinical Significance -- Coronary Perfusion Pressure and Myocardial Infarction
retrieved_docs.content: Early revascularization strategies, such as PCI and thrombolysis, are essential for restoring coronary blood flow and improving CPP. In severe cases, tailored therapies like IABPs and Impella devices can be life-saving, especially when CPP is critically low. Mean arterial pressure (MAP) should be maintained at or greater than 65 to 70 mm Hg. Vasopressors such as norepinephrine can help maintain ADP if hypotension is present. [16] Diuretics or vasodilators like nitroglycerin should be used cautiously to reduce preload without causing hypotension.

retrieved_docs.title: Evaluation and Management of Perioperative Hypertension -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: HTN results in the deposition of atherosclerotic plaque in arteries. Coronary artery disease (CAD), sometimes combined with plaque rupture, leads to myocardial ischemia and infarction. Increased afterload due to HTN increases the works of the heart, resulting in left ventricular hypertrophy (LVH) and CHF. The hypertrophied heart requires more oxygen, but the flow of oxygenated blood is impaired in CAD by luminal narrowing of the coronary arteries. Myocardial infarction results in decreased contractility, which decreases blood flow to the systemic circulation in systole. Coronary blood flow occurs in diastole, and coronary perfusion is decreased in the presence of hypertension-induced LVH because LVH results in decreased compliance and increased left ventricular end-diastolic pressure (LVEDP). Examining the equation for coronary perfusion pressure (CPP), CPP = DBP – LVEDP, makes it readily apparent that increased LVEDP results in decreased coronary blood flow (CBF) in diastole. One way to improve coronary perfusion is to slow the heart rate, which increases the proportion of cardiac cycle time spent in diastole. This explains why tachycardia can result in myocardial ischemia. Although CBF can also be increased by increasing DBP, excessive increases in DBP, whether achieved with volume infusion and/or vasoconstrictors, can result in increased lung water and even APE.

retrieved_docs.title: Myocardial Infarction (Nursing) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Myocardial infarction (MI), colloquially known as “heart attack,” is caused by decreased or complete cessation of blood flow to a portion of the myocardium. Myocardial infarction may be “silent” and go undetected, or it could be a catastrophic event leading to hemodynamic deterioration and sudden death. [1] Most myocardial infarctions are due to underlying coronary artery disease, the leading cause of death in the United States. With coronary artery occlusion, the myocardium is deprived of oxygen. Prolonged deprivation of oxygen supply to the myocardium can lead to myocardial cell death and necrosis. [2] Patients can present with chest discomfort or pressure that can radiate to the neck, jaw, shoulder, or arm. In addition to the history and physical exam, myocardial ischemia may be associated with ECG changes and elevated biochemical markers such as cardiac troponins. [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Timolol -- Contraindications -- Box Warnings (Systemic Beta-Blockers)
retrieved_docs.content: Patients may experience increased sensitivity to catecholamines if β-blocker therapy is discontinued abruptly, potentially leading to worsened angina or myocardial infarction. To minimize risks when stopping chronically administered timolol, particularly for patients with ischemic heart disease, the dosage should be tapered gradually over weeks. If angina significantly worsens or acute coronary insufficiency occurs, timolol should be promptly resumed, and appropriate steps to manage unstable angina should be taken. Patients should be cautioned against discontinuing or altering therapy without consulting a clinician. As coronary artery disease remains commonly underdiagnosed, the abrupt discontinuation of timolol should be avoided, even for patients being treated only for hypertension. [57]

retrieved_docs.title: Posterior Myocardial Infarction -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The reduction of coronary blood flow causes myocardial infarction. Classically this occurs due to atherosclerotic plaque rupture or intraluminal thrombus and is referred to as type I myocardial infarction. However, ischemia may also occur due to either increased oxygen demand or decreased supply and is called type II myocardial infarction; this may be triggered by many conditions such as hypertension, hypotension, coronary artery spasm, anemia, sepsis, and arrhythmias.

retrieved_docs.title: Orthostatic Hypotension -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Although orthostatic hypotension is often asymptomatic or associated with minimal symptoms, it is linked to increased mortality and a higher risk of myocardial infarction, heart failure, stroke, and atrial fibrillation. The elevated vascular mortality associated with orthostatic hypotension may be attributed to its association with cardiac and cerebrovascular diseases, such as myocardial infarctions, transient ischemic attacks, ECG abnormalities, and carotid stenosis. [61] Blood flow through the coronary arteries occurs during diastole; therefore, diastolic hypotension can impair coronary blood flow, placing patients at a particularly elevated risk for cardiovascular events. [62]

retrieved_docs.title: Silent Myocardial Ischemia -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Management strategies address underlying risk factors and improve coronary blood flow through lifestyle modifications, medications (including aspirin, statins, β-blockers, and CCBs), and, in some cases, invasive procedures like angioplasty or CABG. The prognosis depends on factors such as the extent of underlying CAD, treatment effectiveness, and coexisting medical conditions. Early detection and proper management are crucial for improving outcomes and reducing complication risks. Patient education plays a vital role in raising awareness about silent myocardial ischemia, emphasizing the importance of regular medical check-ups, lifestyle modifications, and medication adherence to mitigate complication risks, especially in individuals with cardiovascular risk factors.

retrieved_docs.title: Myocardial Infarction -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Myocardial infarction (MI), colloquially known as “heart attack,” is caused by decreased or complete cessation of blood flow to a portion of the myocardium. Myocardial infarction may be “silent” and go undetected, or it could be a catastrophic event leading to hemodynamic deterioration and sudden death. [1] Most myocardial infarctions are due to underlying coronary artery disease, the leading cause of death in the United States. With coronary artery occlusion, the myocardium is deprived of oxygen. Prolonged deprivation of oxygen supply to the myocardium can lead to myocardial cell death and necrosis. [2] Patients can present with chest discomfort or pressure that can radiate to the neck, jaw, shoulder, or arm. In addition to the history and physical exam, myocardial ischemia may be associated with ECG changes and elevated biochemical markers such as cardiac troponins. [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Coronary Perfusion Pressure -- Clinical Significance -- Pearls
retrieved_docs.content: Key points to remember about CPP's clinical significance include the following: Left ventricular myocardial perfusion primarily occurs during diastole as a consequence of coronary anatomy. CPP is a significant determinant of myocardial oxygen supply, with local regulatory mechanisms maintaining coronary flow across a range of pressures. Acute CPP reduction during circulatory shock can lead to type 2 myocardial infarction, which has a distinct etiology compared to type 1 myocardial infarction. Recognizing the significance of CPP helps distinguish between myocardial infarction types and manage patient outcomes more effectively.

Despite these variations in presentation and vascular involvement, the pathological finding site for all of them is the myocardial tissue, underscoring that myocardial injury is the central feature of acute coronary syndromes regardless of the precise ECG morphology or culprit vessel. Recognizing the specific type of infarction and its associated arterial occlusion aids clinicians in tailoring reperfusion strategies and optimizing patient care while acknowledging that the underlying myocardial damage remains the pivotal concern.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2405
retrieved_docs.content: Acute Q wave infarction – widespread, acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction (including the generic disorder and the specific forms caused by occlusion of the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, the marginal branch of the right coronary artery, the mid‑portion of the right coronary artery, the posterior descending branch of the right coronary artery, and involvement of the left anterior descending coronary artery), acute myocardial infarction due to left coronary artery occlusion, acute myocardial infarction due to occlusion of the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, acute myocardial infarction due to right coronary artery occlusion, acute myocardial infarction occurring during a procedure, acute non‑ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction (disorder), acute widespread myocardial infarction, postoperative acute myocardial infarction, and subsequent anterior non‑ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction are all distinct clinical entities that share a common anatomic focus: the myocardium. Each of these conditions represents an acute loss of blood supply to myocardial tissue, whether the injury is precipitated by specific coronary artery branch occlusions, surgical or procedural contexts, or broader patterns of infarction. Recognizing that all of these varied presentations localize to the myocardium underscores the central role of myocardial injury in the pathophysiology, diagnosis, and management of acute coronary syndromes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 1495
retrieved_docs.content: Coronary artery disease (تصلب الشريان التاجي) is clinically associated with several cardiovascular conditions and diagnostic procedures. It is linked to hypercholesterolemia (فرط كوليسترول الدم), which is a major risk factor for the development of atherosclerotic plaque in the coronary arteries. Coronary artery disease also co‑occurs with congestive heart failure (فشل قلبي احتقاني), reflecting the progression of ischemic damage to cardiac function. Cardiac catheterization (قسطرة قلبية) is frequently employed in the assessment and management of coronary artery disease, as well as in the evaluation of related cardiac disorders (اضطراب قلبي), hypercholesterolemia, chest pain (ألم الصَدْر), and neck pain (ألم في العنق). Cardiac disorders (اضطراب قلبي) themselves show clinical associations with coronary artery disease, hypercholesterolemia, and the use of cardiac catheterization, underscoring the interconnected nature of these pathologies. Chest pain (ألم الصَدْر) is commonly observed in patients with hypercholesterolemia and often prompts cardiac catheterization to rule out or treat underlying coronary artery disease. Neck pain (ألم في العنق) may also be a presenting symptom that leads clinicians to perform cardiac catheterization when cardiac involvement is suspected. Overall, hypercholesterolemia, coronary artery disease, congestive heart failure, various pain presentations, and cardiac catheterization form a tightly linked clinical network, highlighting the importance of integrated diagnostic and therapeutic strategies in cardiovascular patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2399
retrieved_docs.content: Acute myocardial infarction, in its many clinical presentations—including ST‑segment elevation infarctions caused by occlusion of the left anterior descending artery, its septal or proximal branches, the intermediate artery, the mid‑portion of the right coronary artery, the posterior descending or posterior lateral branches of the right coronary artery, as well as non‑ST‑segment elevation infarctions, widespread infarctions, and those occurring during procedures or postoperatively—always involves injury to the myocardium. Regardless of the specific coronary vessel or segment that is blocked, the pathological finding site for each of these myocardial infarction subtypes is the myocardial tissue itself, underscoring that the heart muscle is the central organ affected across the full spectrum of acute infarct presentations. This unified understanding emphasizes that diagnostic evaluation, therapeutic interventions, and patient monitoring should focus on the extent and location of myocardial damage, irrespective of the particular coronary artery involved or the clinical context of the infarction.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2016
retrieved_docs.content: Coronary artery disease (تصلب الشريان التاجي) is clinically associated with a range of cardiac conditions and diagnostic procedures. It is linked to cardiac disorders (اضطراب قلبي), congestive heart failure (فشل قلبي احتقاني), and heart murmurs (نفخة قلبية), reflecting its broad impact on cardiac function and symptomatology. In addition, coronary artery disease is closely connected to the use of cardiac catheterization (قسطرة قلبية) as a key investigative and therapeutic tool. Cardiac disorders and congestive heart failure are also individually associated with cardiac catheterization, underscoring the procedure’s central role in evaluating and managing these interrelated cardiovascular problems. Together, these associations highlight the intertwined nature of coronary artery disease, various cardiac pathologies, and the pivotal role of cardiac catheterization in patient assessment and care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 2559
retrieved_docs.content: Acute Q wave infarction – widespread, acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarctions caused by occlusion of the distal left anterior descending coronary artery, the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, the distal portion of the right coronary artery, the intermediate artery, the marginal branch of the right coronary artery, or the posterior descending branch of the right coronary artery, as well as acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction involving the left anterior descending coronary artery and acute myocardial infarction due to left coronary artery occlusion, all represent distinct presentations of an acute myocardial infarction that share a common pathological substrate in the myocardium. Acute non‑Q wave infarction, acute non‑Q wave infarction – widespread, acute non‑ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction (disorder), acute widespread myocardial infarction, postoperative acute myocardial infarction, and subsequent anterior non‑ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction are additional variants of acute myocardial infarction, each reflecting different electrocardiographic patterns, timing, or clinical contexts, yet each also localizes its injury to the myocardial tissue. Collectively, these entities illustrate how diverse coronary artery occlusions, ECG morphologies, and clinical settings converge on myocardial damage as the central finding site, underscoring the importance of recognizing the specific type of infarction for appropriate diagnosis, risk stratification, and management in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 1103
retrieved_docs.content: Acute myocardial infarction encompasses a spectrum of disorders that all involve injury to the myocardium. Specific forms include Acute Q‑wave infarction – widespread, Acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI), and Acute STEMI due to occlusion of distinct coronary branches such as the left coronary artery, the left anterior descending (LAD) artery, the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, the diagonal branch of the LAD, the intermediate artery, the marginal branch of the right coronary artery, the posterior descending branch of the right coronary artery, and the septal branch of the LAD. Additional variants are Acute myocardial infarction due to left coronary artery occlusion, Acute myocardial infarction due to circumflex branch occlusion, Acute myocardial infarction due to right coronary artery occlusion, Acute myocardial infarction occurring during a procedure, and Acute widespread myocardial infarction. Despite the differing anatomic sites of arterial blockage and electrocardiographic patterns (Q‑wave versus ST‑segment elevation), each of these entities shares the common finding site of the myocardium, underscoring that the primary pathological substrate in all these conditions is myocardial tissue damage. Recognizing the specific coronary artery involved helps guide reperfusion strategies and risk stratification, while the universal involvement of the myocardium highlights the need for prompt diagnosis and treatment to preserve cardiac function.

retrieved_docs.content: occurs iatrogenically, and irrespective of the specific coronary artery or branch that is occluded (including proximal, mid, distal, or branch segments of the left anterior descending, left coronary, circumflex, intermediate, marginal, septal, or right coronary arteries), the affected tissue is the myocardial wall. This consistent anatomic involvement underscores the central role of the myokard in the clinical presentation, diagnostic evaluation, and therapeutic management of all forms of acute myocardial infarction.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 2257
retrieved_docs.content: Acute myocardial infarction is a cardiac event in which the myocardium (myokard) is the primary site of injury, and it can be classified by electrocardiographic patterns, anatomic distribution, and precipitating circumstances. Q‑wave infarctions represent a form of acute MI characterized by pathologic Q waves on the ECG, while ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarctions (STEMI) are identified by elevated ST segments and are further subdivided according to the coronary artery segment that is occluded. Occlusion of the left anterior descending (LAD) artery, including its proximal, mid, distal, septal, and marginal branches, produces anterior‑wall infarctions; blockage of the right coronary artery (RCA) and its branches—such as the posterior descending, marginal, intermediate, and distal portions—results in inferior or right‑sided infarctions; and interruption of the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery leads to lateral‑wall involvement. Non‑ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarctions (NSTEMI) denote a subtype in which ST elevation is absent but myocardial necrosis is present. Widespread or extensive myocardial infarctions describe cases where a large territory of the myocardium is affected, often reflecting multiple or proximal arterial occlusions. Additionally, acute myocardial infarction can occur iatrogenically during invasive cardiac procedures, underscoring the need for vigilant monitoring and rapid intervention. Recognizing the specific coronary artery involved and the ECG pattern guides diagnosis, risk stratification, and therapeutic decisions, thereby improving patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1831
retrieved_docs.content: Coronary artery disease (تصلب الشريان التاجي) is clinically associated with heart murmurs (نفخة قلبية). The presence of a heart murmur can serve as an important clinical indicator of underlying coronary artery pathology, reflecting turbulent blood flow that may result from atherosclerotic narrowing or ischemic changes in the coronary vessels. Recognizing this association helps clinicians consider coronary artery disease in the differential diagnosis when a murmur is detected, prompting further cardiovascular evaluation and appropriate management to reduce the risk of adverse cardiac events.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2726
retrieved_docs.content: اضطراب قلبي, تصلب الشريان التاجي, فشل قلبي احتقاني, التهاب النسيج الخلوي, and قسطرة قلبية are closely interconnected clinical entities. اضطراب قلبي is clinically associated with تصلب الشريان التاجي, indicating that structural abnormalities of the coronary arteries often coexist with or contribute to broader cardiac dysfunction. تصلب الشريان التاجي is further linked to فشل قلبي احتقاني, reflecting that progressive atherosclerotic narrowing of the coronary vessels can lead to impaired myocardial perfusion and subsequent congestive heart failure. قسطرة قلبية is repeatedly noted as a clinical association for اضطراب قلبي, تصلب الشريان التاجي, فشل قلبي احتقاني, and التهاب النسيج الخلوي, underscoring its central role as a diagnostic and interventional tool for evaluating and managing these conditions. The association of التهاب النسيج الخلوي with قسطرة قلبية suggests that inflammatory processes of the cardiac tissue are either assessed during catheter‑based procedures or may represent a complication requiring catheter‑based intervention. Together, these relationships highlight the overlapping pathophysiology of cardiac disorders, coronary artery disease, heart failure, and inflammatory cardiac conditions, and emphasize the pivotal role of قسطرة قلبية in their assessment and treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1153
retrieved_docs.content: Acute myocardial infarction (AMI) is a spectrum of coronary syndromes in which the primary site of injury is the myocardium, manifesting with a characteristic “acute infarct” morphology that includes early necrosis, inflammation and edema of cardiac myocytes. The spectrum encompasses acute Q‑wave infarction (transmural necrosis with a specific Q‑wave morphology), acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI) of various electrocardiographic patterns, acute non‑Q‑wave infarction, acute non‑ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction (NSTEMI), acute widespread myocardial infarction, postoperative acute myocardial infarction, acute myocardial infarction occurring during a procedure, and subsequent anterior non‑ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction. All of these clinical entities are linked to occlusion of specific coronary arterial branches, each producing a localized myocardial injury that corresponds to the territory supplied by the blocked vessel. Reported occlusion sites include the proximal, mid and distal segments of the left anterior descending (LAD) artery, the left coronary artery (LCA) as a whole, the circumflex branch of the LCA, the diagonal and septal branches of the LAD, the intermediate artery, and numerous branches of the right coronary artery (RCA) such as its proximal, mid, distal, marginal, posterior descending, posterior‑lateral and septal branches. Occlusion of any of these vessels generates the hallmark ST‑segment elevation on the electrocardiogram (in STEMI) while the underlying pathology remains confined to myocardial tissue. Recognizing the precise relationship between the anatomic location of coronary blockage, the resulting electrocardiographic pattern and the uniform acute infarct histopathology is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk stratification and timely reperfusion therapy in patients with acute coronary syndromes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 2636
retrieved_docs.content: Acute Q wave infarction – widespread, acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction (including the generic disorder and the numerous subtypes caused by occlusion of the left coronary artery, the anterior descending branch of the left coronary artery, the intermediate artery, the marginal branch of the right coronary artery, the mid‑portion of the right coronary artery, the posterior descending branch of the right coronary artery, the septal branch of the anterior descending branch of the left coronary artery, and the proximal left anterior descending coronary artery), acute myocardial infarction due to left coronary artery occlusion, acute myocardial infarction due to right coronary artery occlusion, acute myocardial infarction occurring during a procedure, acute non‑Q wave infarction, and postoperative acute myocardial infarction all share the same anatomic finding site: the myocardium (myokard). This indicates that regardless of the specific electrocardiographic pattern, coronary artery involved, or clinical context, the primary tissue affected in these acute coronary syndromes is the myocardial muscle. Recognizing the myocardium as the common locus of injury is essential for diagnosis, imaging, and therapeutic strategies aimed at preserving cardiac function across the diverse presentations of acute myocardial infarction.

Together, these entities illustrate that acute infarct encompasses both Q‑wave and non‑Q‑wave patterns and a variety of STEMI presentations determined by the precise location of coronary artery obstruction, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk stratification, and targeted therapeutic management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 2197
retrieved_docs.content: Acute infarct is a cardiac pathology that can manifest with a wide range of morphologic patterns. It is associated with acute Q wave infarction – including the widespread form – and with acute Q wave myocardial infarction. Various forms of acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction are also linked to acute infarct, such as the general disorder and specific presentations caused by occlusion of distinct coronary segments: the anterior descending branch of the left coronary artery, the distal portion of the right coronary artery, the mid portion of the right coronary artery, the posterior descending branch of the right coronary artery, and right coronary artery occlusion overall. In addition, acute infarct encompasses acute myocardial infarction due to occlusion of the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, acute myocardial infarction due to right coronary artery occlusion, and acute myocardial infarction that occurs during a medical procedure. Non‑Q wave morphologies are included as well, with both acute non‑Q wave infarction and its widespread variant being associated with acute infarct. Furthermore, acute infarct is linked to acute non‑ST segment elevation myocardial infarction and to subsequent anterior non‑ST segment elevation myocardial infarction, reflecting its relevance to both initial and later ischemic events. Together, these associations illustrate that acute infarct can present with diverse electrocardiographic and anatomic features, emphasizing the importance of recognizing its various morphologies for accurate diagnosis and management.
